notes.anchor: Dionysius, having come to me beyond my expectation
notes.note: M. Pomponius Dionysius, the learned freedman whom Atticus had given Cicero as tutor to his son and nephew; his desertion in the crisis of 49 BC is a running grievance through book 8, and this is the second of the strained interviews Cicero reports.

notes.anchor: as for the money he had, he did not know where it was
notes.note: Dionysius pleads that his cash is tied up in loans not yet repaid or not yet due, an excuse Cicero plainly disbelieves; the money question is part of the tutor's pretext for not following Cicero into the uncertainty of the war.

notes.anchor: as the boys' teacher, not gladly; as an ungrateful man, not unwillingly
notes.note: Cicero's pointed antithesis: he is sorry to lose the boys' tutor but glad to be rid of an ingrate. The balanced clauses sum up his verdict on Dionysius's desertion.
